content:
Tabular data loading for ML sits at the intersection of **statistics packages**, **databases**, and **data warehouses**.

Early statistical tools (S, R) read rectangular files directly into memory structures—an idea carried into **pandas** (2008), which unified CSV, SQL, and HDF5 access behind `read_*` APIs.

**CSV** dates to the 1970s as a lowest-common-denominator export format. Its simplicity made it the default Kaggle and spreadsheet interchange format, but lack of schema caused decades of dtype bugs.

**Apache Parquet** (2013, Hadoop ecosystem) brought columnar, typed storage to analytics stacks; **PyArrow** integration made Parquet a first-class pandas citizen for large ML datasets.

**JSON** rose with web APIs and log pipelines; **JSON Lines** became standard for streaming events, requiring normalization layers before classic ML.

Modern ML engineering treats loading as a deliberate contract: choose format, dtypes, and columns at the boundary so downstream cleaning and modeling operate on trustworthy, right-sized frames.

timeline.label: Spreadsheet and CSV era
timeline.description: Business data exported as text tables; type information lost at every save.

timeline.label: pandas read API
timeline.description: Single-tool loading from CSV, SQL, JSON, and HDF5 with dtype and parse hooks.

timeline.label: Columnar analytics formats
timeline.description: Parquet and Arrow reduce I/O and preserve schemas for large-scale training pipelines.
